bio: Reyhaneh Hosseinpourkhoshkbari is a fourth-year PhD student in Cognition and Neuroscience at the University of Texas at Dallas, advised by Dr. Richard Golden in the Coins Lab. She earned a BS in Statistics from Shahid Beheshti University in Tehran, Iran, and an MS in Applied Cognition and Neuroscience at UT Dallas. Her training spans generative AI and large language models, statistics, machine learning, and psychometrics, and she has served as a teaching assistant for courses in neural networks, computational modeling in Python, and statistics for psychology.

Her research centers on large-language-model-based virtual patients for medical education, with the goal of building simulated patients that are clinically grounded, behaviorally diverse, and educationally meaningful. Recent projects include realism evaluation of LLM-based virtual patients (via expert review, Turing tests, and linguistic analysis), personality-driven patient simulation, and the integration of Cognitive Diagnostic Models to assess clinical reasoning. She has also collaborated with her advisor on new misspecification tests for Cognitive Diagnostic Models, presented at the Annual Meeting of the Psychometric Society, and worked on personality prediction from text, emotion detection, and RAG/LLM fine-tuning. Her recent work has appeared at the Machine Learning for Health (ML4H) and NeurIPS GenAI4Health workshops.

For her proposed research, she aims to develop a unified framework for LLM-based virtual patients that combines multi-level realism evaluation, controllable personality-driven behavior, and psychometric assessment of student–patient interactions to support scalable training in clinical reasoning and communication.

As an international PhD student, she hopes the summer camp will offer guidance, perspective, and meaningful connections to grow academically and professionally. She is especially eager for the collaborative environment — to receive detailed feedback on her work on evaluating and applying virtual patients in healthcare education, to contribute actively to others' projects, and to engage with peers from different backgrounds whose interdisciplinary perspectives can reveal new research directions.
tagline: LLM virtual patients for clinical training

bio: Ayush Noori is a D.Phil. student in Engineering Science at the University of Oxford, where he is a Rhodes Scholar and Encode: AI for Science Fellow in the Computational Health Informatics (Clinical Machine Learning) group led by Prof. David Clifton. He completed his A.B. in Computer Science and Neuroscience (Phi Beta Kappa) and a Master's in Computer Science at Harvard University, and previously worked in Prof. Marinka Zitnik's lab at Harvard Medical School. He conducts research at the interface of artificial intelligence, neuroscience, and precision medicine, with a stated mission of developing AI technologies that expand personalized diagnostic and therapeutic possibilities for neurological and other challenging diseases. He reports an extensive research record, including 40+ publications (14 as first or co-first author) across venues such as Cell, Nature Neuroscience, Nature Machine Intelligence, and NEJM AI, and recognition including the Rhodes and Barry Goldwater Scholarships.

His proposed research, TrialAgent, is an AI system for closed-loop, agentic clinical discovery from population-scale health records. It formulates hypotheses as target trials and combines EHR data with external biomedical evidence (omics, literature, experimental protocols) to prioritize hypotheses testable in both observational data and experimental systems, then dispatches subagents to build cohorts, adjust for confounders, and run causal analyses, iterating until a hypothesis is revised, retested, or nominated for prospective validation. The goal is to improve research productivity by intelligently allocating expensive validation investment, with early prototypes evaluated on synthetic EHRs and planned large-scale evaluation across multiple health systems.

Ayush is active in the ML-for-health community, having served on the ML4H organizing committee (Co-Chair of Demos and Perspectives in 2024, Chair of Logistics in 2025). His long-term goal is to lead an independent research lab advancing knowledge-grounded AI for neurology. At the AHLI Summer Camp, he hopes to share his work and receive interdisciplinary feedback on his proposed research program, gain training in the regulatory, legal, and operational landscape of health-AI deployment and data partnerships, and connect with a broader cohort of emerging leaders through the workshops, small-group discussions, and team-building days.
tagline: Agentic AI for closed-loop clinical discovery

bio: Seyedeh Somayyeh Mousavi is a fourth-year PhD candidate in Computer Science and Bioinformatics in the Department of Biomedical Informatics at Emory University School of Medicine, where she is advised by Dr. Reza Sameni in the Alphanumerics Lab. Her doctoral work centers on demographic-aware artificial intelligence for cardiovascular risk prediction, using both longitudinal and cross-sectional analyses of electrocardiogram (ECG) and electronic health record data. Before joining Emory, she earned an M.Sc. in Biomedical Engineering (Bioelectric) and a B.Sc. in Electrical Engineering from the University of Zanjan in Iran, with a long-standing focus on biomedical signal processing and cuff-less blood pressure estimation.

Her research spans machine learning and deep learning analysis of ECGs in congenital heart disease, the development of open-source ECG feature-extraction tooling, and large-scale studies of bias in blood pressure measurement—including a population-wide analysis of more than 75 million blood pressure encounters from Emory Healthcare. She has published in venues such as Physiological Measurement, MDPI Sensors, and the IEEE EMBC conference, and has been recognized with awards including the IEEE EMBC NextGen Scholar Award and multiple best-thesis honors.

Mousavi's stated long-term goal is to build an academic career as a faculty member and establish an independent research laboratory advancing equitable, data-driven approaches in cardiology and cardiovascular medicine. She is particularly interested in robust AI models that integrate cross-sectional and longitudinal health data to improve early detection, risk stratification, and personalized management of cardiovascular disease. She hopes the AHLI Summer Camp will help her broaden her interdisciplinary perspective by engaging with leading researchers in AI, bioengineering, and clinical science, and will help her refine her vision for building impactful, clinically translatable research programs in academia.

bio: Benjamin Fox is a PhD candidate (Biomedical Sciences, Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technologies training area) at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, advised by Girish N. Nadkarni and Ankit Parekh in the Augmented Intelligence in Medicine and Science / Sleep and Circadian Analysis (SCAN) groups. His dissertation focuses on learning latent representations of human physiological signals to assess clinical states and disease risk in sleep and critical care. He earned a BS in Physics (with a CS minor) from Pepperdine University and an MS in Computer Science from UC Santa Barbara, and he previously worked as a data scientist at Evidation Health and as a machine learning engineer at Musubi.

His research builds foundational transformer and joint-embedding (JEPA-style) models for physiological time-series data — including full-night, multichannel polysomnography for sleep staging and cardiovascular risk, and bedside monitoring data for real-time hypotension and cardiac arrest risk estimation. He is the first author of SleepJEPA and PhysioJEPA, and his sleep foundation model work (sometimes referred to as PFTSleep) drew media coverage as one of the largest studies of its kind. His broader interests span time series, wearables, omics, and natural language processing for health and social good.

For the AHLI summer camp, Benjamin proposes identifying multimodal sleep-signal features (EEG, PPG, ECG, sleep stage, and body position from polysomnography) that are associated with glucose control, pairing PSG with continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data to better identify people at risk for metabolic disease.

He is excited to attend for networking and potential postdoc collaborations, to share his current work and receive feedback from experts at the intersection of AI and health (especially in time series, wearables, and omics), and to learn additional techniques. He is also keen to participate in discussions on deployment of AI systems in health settings, AI safety, AI environmental/climate impacts, and AI policy.
tagline: Foundation models for sleep and physiological signals

name: Alexander Schubert
awards: Eric and Wendy Schmidt Center Fellowship, Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard (2022-2024)
Berkeley Fellowship, UC Berkeley (2021-2023)
German National Academic Foundation (Studienstiftung) (2017-2019)
DAAD Graduate Scholarship (2017-2019)
Dean's List (best 1%), Goethe University Frankfurt

highlights.title: New Program Trains Data Scientists Who are Transforming Medicine

bio: Alexander Schubert is a PhD student in Computational Precision Health, a joint program between UC Berkeley and UCSF, supervised by Ziad Obermeyer and Ahmed Alaa. He holds a BSc in Economics and Business Administration from Goethe University Frankfurt (with distinction) and, before his PhD, spent several years as a senior associate at McKinsey & Company in Munich leading data-science teams on healthcare and pharmaceutical projects. He has been a Berkeley Fellow and a Fellow of the Eric and Wendy Schmidt Center at the Broad Institute, and he interned as an AI research intern at Dandelion Health.

His research focuses on the development and responsible real-world deployment of machine learning for high-stakes cardiovascular prediction, centered on deep learning applied to ECG waveforms linked to large-scale clinical outcome data. His work spans three streams: an ECG model for sudden cardiac death risk now in prospective multi-site deployment in Sweden, the U.S., and Taiwan; a handheld single-lead ECG screen for silent prior myocardial infarction undergoing randomized validation in rural Tamil Nadu with the state Department of Health and J-PAL; and an ECG-based model for detecting drug-induced hERG-channel inhibition for cardiac drug safety. His publications appear in or are under review at venues including Nature, NEJM AI, Nature Communications, ICLR, and IEEE JBHI.

Through the summer camp, Alexander hopes to engage with the questions that most occupy him at the intersection of methodology and real-world use: designing architectures and training setups suited to clinical data, evaluating models so they hold up under prospective deployment, and translating strong retrospective performance into tools that change care. He sees the curriculum as spanning exactly this range and hopes to learn from instructors and a capable cohort of peers, and to come away with a community he can keep building with long after the week ends.
tagline: Data science for precision cardiovascular health

name: Tae Jones
awards: Best Paper Award, ACM CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (2024) — MigraineTracker
T32 Biomedical and Health Informatics Postdoctoral Fellowship, University of Washington
TL1 Translational Research Training Program Trainee, Institute of Translational Health Sciences (2023-2024)
bio: Tae Jones is a graduating fifth-year PhD candidate in Computer Science & Engineering at the University of Washington's Paul G. Allen School, co-advised by Dr. James Fogarty and Dr. Sean Munson and a member of the DUB group. Working at the intersection of Health Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) and digital health, she studies how structural and social factors—including implicit bias based on race, gender, socioeconomic background, and mental health stigma—shape health outcomes for patients managing physical-mental comorbidities. She came to the field through a BBA in Information Systems (with an Applied Statistics minor) and an MS in Healthcare Management & Informatics from Kennesaw State University, along with prior industry experience as a data analyst.

Her doctoral work spans user-centered, evidence-based digital health projects across cancer, depression, irritable bowel syndrome, hypertension, and migraine, in collaboration with partners including Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center, Kaiser Permanente Washington, and the UW School of Nursing. She led deployment interviews for Project SCOPE, a study of technology-enhanced collaborative care management of depression in cancer settings (CSCW 2024), and contributed to the MigraineTracker study examining patient experiences with goal-directed self-tracking (CHI 2024, a Best Paper awardee). Her first-authored CHI 2025 Late-Breaking Work examined researcher experiences and tensions around participant engagement in Health HCI research, and her dissertation investigates why patients withdraw from health technology studies. She completed the ITHS TL1 Translational Research Training Program and secured a competitive T32 Biomedical and Health Informatics Postdoctoral Fellowship to continue at UW with Dr. Andrea Hartzler, where her proposed research investigates why clinicians disengage from AI-generated bias-feedback tools like ConverSense and what design interventions can sustain that engagement.

At the AHLI Summer Camp, Tae hopes to deepen her technical fluency in machine learning for healthcare so she can translate her insights about social determinants of health into operationalized AI systems, and to learn how to frame health-equity questions in ways that are tractable for ML approaches without losing their nuance. She is also eager to build lasting cross-disciplinary collaborations—particularly with researchers and practitioners working on clinical study design, patient engagement, and real-world implementation—and to expand her network into communities complementary to her own as she transitions from her dissertation into a postdoctoral research agenda.
tagline: Human-centered AI for health equity

name: Tiffany Hsieh
affiliation: Brown University School of Public Health
awards: Data Science Institute Collaborative Grant (2025)
Selected for 2026 SER Mid-Year Student Dissertation Workshop (2025)
Nominated for Google PhD Fellowship (2025)
Brown-Sanofi Predoctoral Fellowship (2024-2025)
Oral Presentation Merit Award (2016)
bio: Tiffany (Han-Chih) Hsieh is a third-year PhD student in Epidemiology at the Brown University School of Public Health, concurrently completing a Master of Arts in Biostatistics, advised by Rebecca A. Hubbard and Sarah F. Ackley. She earned an ScM in Epidemiology (General Epidemiology and Methodology) and a Certificate in Pharmacoepidemiology and Drug Safety from the Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, and a BS in Biomedical Imaging and Radiological Sciences from National Yang-Ming University in Taiwan. Her research interests span causal inference, epidemiologic methods, biostatistics, pharmacoepidemiology, comparative effectiveness research, and oncology.

As a Brown-Sanofi Predoctoral Fellow, Tiffany has developed and evaluated machine learning models (LASSO, random forest, gradient boosting, neural networks) to predict surveillance "false alarms" in breast cancer survivors using high-dimensional EHR data, co-led a multi-cohort heart failure study using marginal structural models, and conducted simulation studies of selection bias under alternative causal diagrams. She has also interned at Merck's Biostatistics and Research Decision Sciences group, where she designed target trial emulation simulations for HPV vaccine effectiveness and standardized R workflows for FDA-facing analytic reporting. Her dissertation integrates transformer-based representation learning with advanced causal inference methods to compare adjuvant chemotherapy regimens for older women with early-stage breast cancer using SEER-Medicare data.

Tiffany hopes the summer camp will give her deeper grounding in the methodological frontier—particularly how leading groups approach ML-based confounding adjustment, deployment-aware evaluation, and foundation models on EHR and claims data—as she works at the intersection of ML and causal inference, where she has felt the methodological tension between prediction-optimized ML and the assumptions causal estimation requires. She also seeks direct mentorship and feedback from faculty who have shaped the field and a community of peers working on adjacent problems, noting that PhD work in this niche can feel isolating. Beyond research, she is an active community leader, serving as president of the Brown Graduate Consulting Club.
tagline: Causal ML for cancer treatment decisions

name: Sanjana Ramprasad
awards: Khoury Graduate Fellowship Award, Northeastern University (2020)
bio: Sanjana Ramprasad is a final-year PhD candidate in Computer Science at Northeastern University, advised by Byron C. Wallace in the Khoury College NLP group; she expects to graduate shortly and begin a postdoctoral appointment (at Stanford). She earned an MSc in Computer Science from SUNY Buffalo (working on handwriting verification with CNNs) and a BE in Information Science from PESIT, and previously worked as a machine learning engineer at Mya Systems before her PhD. During her PhD she interned multiple times at Abridge AI, working with Zachary Lipton and Elisa Ferracane.

Her research is in natural language processing for healthcare, with a focus on identifying and mitigating hallucinations in large language models, especially for clinical applications. Her thesis examines reliable summarization through the lens of factuality, hallucination patterns, and metrics. Representative work includes TrialsSummarizer for medical evidence synthesis (EACL 2023 demo), evaluating the factuality of zero-shot summarizers across domains (EACL 2024), analyzing circumstantial hallucination trends in dialogue summarization (ACL 2024), and a critical evaluation of automatic factuality metrics (NeurIPS 2025). She is a recipient of the Khoury Graduate Fellowship.

Her summer camp research statement proposes using mechanistic interpretability to probe and steer an LLM's internal representation of "factual strictness," enabling context-aware, user-controlled tolerance for confabulation, so that models can be held to strict factuality in high-stakes settings while allowing plausible inferences where appropriate. Because she plans to graduate within a month and start a postdoc in AI/healthcare, she hopes the camp's guidance and mentorship will help her scope projects and plan her postdoctoral research on making LLMs more reliable, interpretable, and controllable for use by healthcare experts. She also hopes to refine her broader vision for how factuality should be measured, detected, and controlled in healthcare, and to find potential collaborators across CS/AI and healthcare.
tagline: Curbing hallucination in medical language models

name: Dipendra Pant
affiliation: Norwegian University of Science and Technology
awards: University Grant Commission and Kathmandu University Collaboration Scholarship (Master's)
Master's Thesis Research Grant, Kathmandu University
Semester Topper Scholarship (undergraduate studies)
bio: Dipendra Pant is a fourth-year (final-year) PhD student in Computer Science at the Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU) in Trondheim, where he specializes in data science and clinical decision support systems. His doctoral work is part of the project "Data-driven decision aids for Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS)" and the larger IDDEAS initiative, which develops and evaluates decision aids for children with developmental disorders in collaboration with regional CAMHS clinics and research groups across the US and Europe. Before his PhD, he earned an ME in Computer Engineering from Kathmandu University and a BE in Computer Engineering from Pokhara University in Nepal, and he worked for several years as a web/front-end developer and instructor in Nepal.

His research centers on applying causal inference, predictive modeling, and longitudinal EHR analysis to child and adolescent mental health. He has published work visualizing patient trajectories and disorder co-occurrences, forecasting readmission risk, and establishing frameworks for the secondary use of health records in treatment planning, alongside contributions on synthetic data generation for privacy. He brings a broad technical background spanning machine learning frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn), full-stack web development, and cloud/DevOps tooling, and has authored a book on statistics for data scientists.

For the proposed project, Dipendra aims to move clinical AI from predicting crises to prescribing solutions, developing a "causal trajectory alignment" tool that uses counterfactual inference and conditional average treatment effects to recommend interventions most likely to stabilize a patient's trajectory.

At the summer camp, he hopes to learn about approaching clinical decision-making, how multimodal data and the data-generation process shape problem solvability, the practical design and evaluation of causal inference models from the speakers, and navigating funding across government, industry, and philanthropy.
tagline: Causal decision support for youth mental health
